concern_text: The alg-header verification scheme is the RFC 8725 anti-pattern (fact 2): RS256->HS256 algorithm confusion using the public key as the HMAC secret; rejecting alg:none is necessary but not sufficient. Pin algorithm per key/issuer.

The alg-header step directly contradicts pack #2 — reading-and-following the header is exactly what RFC 8725 says not to do; same class as #9, enabling RS256/HS256 algorithm-confusion via the public key as HMAC secret. Pin algorithm to kid/issuer.

Reading the alg header contradicts pack item 2 (RFC 8725) and is CWE-347; mixed RS256/HS256 fleet is the canonical precondition for algorithm/key confusion (public RSA key used as HMAC secret). Nullifies the auth scheme.

"Detect theft on reuse / limit blast radius" overreaches: rotation (item 5) detects only refresh-token replay; a leaked access token stays valid the full 10-15 min with no revocation path in a stateless model (CWE-613). Revocation/introspection unaddressed.

"Terminate TLS at the gateway" can violate item 7 (RFC 6750 §5.3) if the forwarded token traverses a plaintext internal segment; "terminate at gateway" + "HTTPS end-to-end" is internally contradictory.
Pack-accuracy note on item 1: "claims are protected by a signature" overstates RFC 7519 — a JWT may be a JWS or JWE and the unsigned alg:none form exists; signing is not intrinsic to the format.

Salt derived by hashing the email is a predictable salt = CWE-760; email is public/deterministic, contradicts the OWASP Cheat Sheet's CSPRNG requirement, and is additionally CWE-330; per pack item 3 the rainbow-table defense is materially weakened for targeted accounts.

Session fixation (CWE-384): preserving the pre-auth identifier across the privilege boundary violates OWASP's 'Renew the Session ID After Any Privilege Level Change'; regenerate on login and migrate session data.

CSRF under-specified (CWE-352): SameSite=Lax is the only CSRF control; OWASP CSRF Prevention Cheat Sheet treats SameSite as defense-in-depth, requiring a synchronizer/double-submit token for payment flows.
'Random' identifier is not stated to be a CSPRNG (CWE-330/CWE-338); a 128-bit value from a non-CSPRNG is predictable regardless of length.

Restricting types by checking the multipart Content-Type header contradicts pack items 1-2 (header is client-set/forgeable) and re-introduces the CWE-434 risk of item 3; fix is magic-byte sniffing (item 8).

Type-validation via the client Content-Type header is the exact bypass items 1-3 warn against (item 2: arbitrarily forgeable; item 1: OWASP says validate by content; item 3: CWE-434); header check has zero security value vs an attacker.

The headline type-gate trusts the Content-Type header that OWASP/CWE-434/fact 2 say is attacker-controlled; an attacker scripts the multipart request and ships a PHP/SVG/polyglot labeled image/png, making the gate decorative and worse than no check (false confidence).
The recommendation never questions whether to accept raw uploads at all; for avatars, Gravatar/identicon/initials or a managed image service dodges the entire CWE-434 blast radius.

Type validation via the Content-Type header contradicts facts 1-3 (client-set/forged per fact 2, OWASP content-based per fact 1, CWE-434 bypass per fact 3); change to magic-byte signatures (fact 8) or re-encode (fact 9).

Pack validity coefficients rest entirely on Schmidt & Hunter (1998) and ignore Sackett, Zhang, Berry & Lievens (2022, JAP), which argues the range-restriction corrections were overstated and reports GMA ≈ .31 and structured interviews ≈ .42 — flipping the ranking so the interview, not GMA, is the stronger predictor; this undermines any 'lead with GMA' logic.

The .51 validities are presented as settled but contested: they are operational (corrected) values, and Sackett et al. (2022) put GMA's corrected validity materially lower (~.3 range), so citing only the 1998 figures overstates expected real-world gain.
